episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Indigenous Lands & Innovation
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Indigenous Lands & Innovation.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [CBC Kamloops] Sacred fire at Williams Lake First Nation marks 5 years since Tk'emlúps te Secwépemc announcement and [IndigiNews] In Kjipuktuk, Indigenous photojournalism fellows end week with emotion-filled ceremony, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: What does it take for Indigenous communities to build and control their own technology infrastructure rather than relying on external vendors and advisory roles? Topics include: Data sovereignty infrastructure ownership vs. advisory roles, OCAP principles and the gap between endorsement and implementation, Indigenous AI governance: advisory circles and decision-making authority, Cultural knowledge encoding in technology and intellectual property limitations, Shared vs. individual community infrastructure models and accountability structures.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Quesnel Tillicum Society / Native Friendship Centre

articles.title: Māori-owned data storage network hailed as significant step towards data sovereignty
articles.summary: Te Pā Tūwatawata will be available to marae, hapū, iwi or other organisations who wish to store their data within the protection of the Pā.

articles.title: Preserving Kwakwaka'wakw carving traditions through robotics and applied learning
articles.summary: Researchers from Camosun College and the University of Victoria have been awarded a grant to support the preservation of Northwest Coast Indigenous carving traditions through an adaptive robotic syste...

discussion.central_question: What does it take for Indigenous communities to build and control their own technology infrastructure rather than relying on external vendors and advisory roles?
discussion.riley_position: Indigenous communities need to move from principle-endorsement (like OCAP) to actual infrastructure ownership, with the Māori data network as a buildable model that shared regional infrastructure through coordinating bodies like the First Nations Technology Council could replicate.
riley_key_evidence: Te Pā Tūwatawata: Māori-owned data storage network with actual servers and governance control, not just policy commitments
First Nations Technology Council in BC has been building shared technical infrastructure for years and could coordinate a multi-nation data infrastructure proposal
Carving robotics project could be improved with pre-deployment community IP agreements and restrictions on commercial deployment without Nation consent
discussion.casey_position: The gap between principle and infrastructure is real and resource-intensive, and the risks of underfunded half-measures, unclear accountability, and encoding cultural knowledge into systems without proper governance protections are substantial and cannot be solved by technology alone.
casey_key_evidence: OCAP principles are well-cited but most communities lack actual technical infrastructure matching those commitments
Advisory bodies without decision rights are legitimacy mechanisms, not governance mechanisms, and can allow companies to claim consultation without genuine power-sharing
Carving traditions involve cultural transmission protocols that predate IP frameworks and cannot be easily encoded into grant agreements or licenses
discussion.resolution: Casey and Riley converged on the structural funding problem as the real bottleneck: Indigenous communities need operational funding for multi-year governance capacity around technology infrastructure, not just project funding tied to funder timelines. They agreed governance and resources—not technology—are the actual limiting factors.
topics_covered: Data sovereignty infrastructure ownership vs. advisory roles
OCAP principles and the gap between endorsement and implementation
Indigenous AI governance: advisory circles and decision-making authority
Cultural knowledge encoding in technology and intellectual property limitations
Shared vs. individual community infrastructure models and accountability structures
